question: Why does N=64 accuracy test fail at 17.5% error (threshold 5%)? Is this a code bug or parameter mismatch?
findings: MTT is entirely novel to prolix — no reference implementations in OpenMM or GROMACS exist
Full Gram-Schmidt re-orthogonalization (mtt_logdet.py:174–179) implemented correctly per Golub & Meurant theory (two passes against all k basis vectors)
N=64 test uses n_probes=10, n_lanczos=20 (fast/low-accuracy configuration). Production target is n_probes=20, n_lanczos=50.
Hutchinson variance formula (mtt_theory.md:39) predicts std_error ≈ sqrt(2/K) ≈ 45% for K=10 probes. Observed 17.5% error is WITHIN theoretical bounds.
5% accuracy gate is unrealistic for n_probes=10 parameter configuration. Theory suggests 10–20% error is expected at this scale.
No code bugs detected. Issue is parameter/expectation mismatch, not implementation error.
open_questions: What accuracy does N=64 achieve with production parameters (n_probes=20, n_lanczos=50)? Expected: <5%, confirming gate is realistic only for full config.
Is feature matrix conditioning issue causing error? (unlikely; no ill-conditioning detected)
Could Lanczos convergence be accelerated with different polynomial basis? (research question, not a bug)
